data.title: Video-generation boom sees China’s Kling AI post US$240 million in annual revenue run rate
data.description:
Generative artificial intelligence service Kling AI in December recorded more than US$20 million in sales, which corresponded to an annualised revenue run rate (ARR) of US$240 million, according to Chinese operator Kuaishou Technology.
That was more than double Kling AI’s US$100 million ARR in March last year, according to a statement on Tuesday from Bejing-based Kuaishou.
ARR is a financial metric used to project a company’s 12-month revenue by extrapolating earnings from a shorter period, like a month or a quarter.
Kling AI’s commercial progress was attributed to Kuaishou’s steady upgrade of the service with features that addressed the diverse requirements of AI video creators around the world, according to a report on Tuesday by Fitch Ratings.
Some of the world’s most popular generative AI services, such as those of video platform HeyGen and music tools provider Suno AI, last year reached the US$100 million ARR level.
Kuaishou – the short video platform rival in China of ByteDance-owned Douyin – said Kling AI’s innovative features have led to widespread adoption in the fields of marketing and e-commerce, as well as in films, television and animation.
That feat underscored how the monetisation of AI video tools worldwide gathered pace and Kuaishou’s efforts to develop one of the world’s most powerful video generation systems, rivalling OpenAI’s Sora and Google DeepMind’s Veo.

Consultancy Artificial Analysis recently ranked Kling AI as the world’s top model for image-to-video generation and second in the text-to-video category.
Kuaishou introduced the latest generation of Kling AI in April amid increased global competition in AI video-generating services.
At that time, the Kling product range had more than 22 million global users, who had generated over 168 million video clips and 344 million images, according to Kuaishou senior vice-president Gai Kun.
Last month, Kuaishou launched a suite of new models including the world’s first unified multimodal video model, Kling Video O1, and the latest iteration of video tool Kling Video 2.6.
Under a single platform, those products supported a range of complex editing functions – such as swapping in characters, editing out backgrounds, retouching partial video content – while everything else was kept intact and other details were fine-tuned.
According to Kuaishou, more than 60 million creators worldwide used Kling products at the end of 2025 and generated more than 600 video clips.
In a November research note, analysts at China Merchants Securities International credited improved video quality and reduced cost for the widespread use of AI video-generating tools across the global entertainment and advertising industries.

data.title: South Korea-Japan summit exposes ‘strategic’ divide amid great-power tensions
data.description:
The summit between South Korea and Japan has exposed a growing divergence in how the two neighbours are responding to a more volatile regional order, as supply chains harden into strategic tools and great-power rivalry sharpens, analysts have said.
During a meeting in Nara, the hometown and electoral district of Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi, South Korean President Lee Jae Myung pressed the case for broader regional cooperation that included China, while Tokyo emphasised closer alignment with Washington and a tighter security partnership among US allies.
The talks followed Lee’s meeting with Chinese President Xi Jinping in Beijing a week earlier. The pair had last year also held discussions on the sidelines of the Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation forum in Gyeongju in October and the G20 summit in South Africa in November.
The latest summit took place against the backdrop of China’s move to restrict exports of certain strategic materials to Japan, a step widely interpreted by analysts as retaliation for Takaichi’s remarks suggesting Tokyo could militarily intervene in the event of a conflict over Taiwan.

Although rare earths were not explicitly mentioned in the joint press release, analysts said supply chain risks were likely discussed behind closed doors, given the knock-on effects for South Korea’s export-driven economy.
The two leaders agreed to deepen inter-ministerial discussions to pursue “strategic and mutually beneficial cooperation in the areas of economic security”, Takaichi said.
“I had in-depth discussions with the president on supply chain cooperation,” she said.
Yang Ki-ho, a professor of Japan studies at Sungkonghoe University, noted that the focus on economic security and supply chains suggested China’s rare earth shipment restrictions had been on the agenda.
During the summit, Lee also emphasised the importance of “South Korea-China-Japan cooperation” for regional peace and stability amid rapidly changing international circumstances, even as Takaichi conspicuously omitted China and repeatedly called for stronger cooperation among Japan, South Korea and the United States.
“I also emphasised the need for the three countries of South Korea, China and Japan in Northeast Asia to find common ground as much as possible, and to communicate and cooperate together,” he added.

Yang noted that the summit highlighted differing priorities, with Seoul seeking to balance relations with China and Japan under Lee’s “pragmatist diplomacy”, while Tokyo aimed to draw South Korea closer to its strategic orbit.
“Japan is focused on sharing strategic security interests with South Korea as it faces frozen ties with China and a weakening alliance with the US under President [Donald] Trump’s mercenary, transactional diplomacy,” he told This Week in Asia.
Seoul is also keen to boost economic cooperation with Tokyo, including its bid to join the Japan-led Comprehensive and Progressive Agreement for Trans-Pacific Partnership (CPTPP).
South Korea has so far been denied membership in the bloc, with Japan calling for the lifting of Seoul’s ban on fisheries products from Fukushima prefecture, home to tsunami-damaged nuclear reactors.
“South Korea’s membership in the CPTPP is crucial for its economic future as the global economy is being reorganised into separate economic blocs,” said Lee Won-deog, a professor at Kookmin University.


The two countries also reaffirmed their commitment to the “complete denuclearisation of the Korean peninsula and the establishment of lasting peace”, and agreed to maintain close coordination on North Korea policy.
This prompted Kim Yo-jong, the powerful sister of North Korean leader Kim Jong-un, to quickly condemn the South Korean president for “soliciting favours” abroad, while reaffirming Pyongyang’s stance of the two Koreas as hostile states rather than a divided nation awaiting reunification.
“No matter how much the person in power travels abroad to solicit favours, no matter how much the authorities pretend to be good-natured and dream big, the reality of the Republic of Korea and the DPRK will never change,” she said, referring to South Korea and North Korea respectively.
Kim also demanded an official apology from Seoul over drone incursions in 2024 allegedly ordered by impeached former president Yoon Suk-yeol to provoke the North and justify his short-lived imposition of martial law.

Yang Moo-jin, a professor at the University of North Korean Studies, called the timing of the statement significant.
“By releasing a statement immediately after the Korea-Japan summit, the North indirectly expressed its opposition to efforts by Japan and South Korea to pursue denuclearisation and peace on the Korean peninsula,” he said.
Leif-Eric Easley, a professor of international studies at Ewha Womans University in Seoul, said North Korea was not interested in conciliatory gestures from Seoul.
“It worries about its own strength in the face of South Korea’s advancing military technologies, alliance with the US, and soft power, which could threaten the legitimacy of its rights-abusing and economically weaker system,” Easley said.
“This is why Kim Yo-jong has resurfaced to make threats and allegations about new drone flights into North Korea.”
Analysts also note that South Korea is being courted by both Japan and China amid simmering regional tensions, citing Lee’s warm reception in Beijing a week earlier.
Xi had said China and South Korea must firmly stand “on the right side of history and make the correct strategic choices” amid intensifying rivalry among major powers – a thinly veiled call for Seoul to align more closely with Beijing.
Relations between South Korea and Japan have long been strained by territorial disputes over Dokdo, known as Takeshima in Japan, and unresolved historical grievances stemming from Japan’s 1910-45 colonial rule, including compensation for victims of wartime forced labour.

Setting aside these thorny issues, Lee and Takaichi focused on what Lee Won-deog described as “low-hurdle issues”.
These included cooperation on recovering and identifying remains from the Chosei undersea coal mine in Japan’s Yamaguchi prefecture, where a flood in 1942 killed 183 workers, including 136 Korean forced labourers.
The two sides also agreed to launch working-level consultations on artificial intelligence and intellectual property rights, and strengthen joint responses to transnational crimes such as widespread online scams.
“Amid a complex and dizzying international order, I believe that cooperation between Korea and Japan is more important than ever,” Lee said at the start of the summit.
He called on both countries to build a better future despite unresolved historical issues.
“Geopolitical uncertainty is growing, multilateralism is being tested, and interdependence within global supply chains is being weaponised,” Lee said.
“To address the pressing challenges we face, I hope the two countries will squarely confront history while expanding cooperation based on deep mutual trust.”

data.title: China factory production error gives toy horses’ mouths pouty curves, wins hearts, boosts sales
data.description:
A production blunder at a factory in eastern China has turned toy ponies into viral crying-faced horses which have become popular mascots for the Year of the Horse.
In January, a netizen shared a picture of a pony with a crying face and the retailer, amused by the mistake, offered to exchange it.
The toy was made in Yiwu International Trade City, which is known as the world’s largest wholesale market for small commodities.

The 20cm tall horse which costs 25 yuan (US$4), symbolises the Year of the Horse and is red for good luck. It also features a golden “money comes quickly” message embroidered on its body.
According to mainland media outlet People’s Daily, a sewing error caused the toy’s pouty mouth and nostrils facing downwards, creating a crying face that was originally seen as a defect.
However, many netizens have embraced the imperfection, dubbing the flawed toy “Crying Horse”.

A buyer, known online as Tuan Tuan Mami, said online: “This little horse looks so sad and pitiful, just like the way I feel at work.
“With this crying toy in the Year of the Horse, I hope to leave all my grievances at work behind and keep only happiness.”
Many young people, who often call themselves niu ma, a euphemistic term for overworked labourers in China meaning “cattle and horse”, saw the crying toy as the new symbol of office workers.

Another buyer said the crying horses were more relatable than their smiling counterparts.
“Imperfect is the true reflection of ordinary people. Our flaws are what make life truly vibrant,” the person wrote.
Factory owner Zhang Huoqing told the media that after the toy horse’s release last October, daily sales were just 400 units.

However, following the viral success of the crying version, she now receives tens of thousands of orders daily.
To meet the surge, the factory quickly added more than 10 production lines.
Though the worker responsible for the mistake remains unknown, Zhang has promised to reward the entire production team.
The company has reportedly applied for an appearance patent for the crying-faced horse and expanded into merchandise like keychains and travel neck pillows.
Zhang also mentioned a surge in international wholesale orders, including from South Africa, the Middle East and Southeast Asia.

The crying horse quickly became an online sensation, with related topics on mainland social media garnering more than a billion views.
February 17 marks the start of the Year of the Horse in the Chinese calendar. In Chinese tradition, the horse symbolises vitality, progress and resilience.
One person said: “The crying horse is the perfect mascot for 2026. I hope we can all embrace life’s sadness and adversity with ease.”
“One worker’s mistake led to an unexpected but cute outcome,” said another.
While a third wrote: “What is even more impressive is the retailer’s acute insight and the Chinese factory’s rapid response.”

data.title: Meet Rebecca Rittenhouse, who plays Lexy in Netflix’s His & Hers
data.description:
Netflix’s latest psychological thriller series His & Hers, adapted from Alice Feeney’s 2020 book of the same name and starring Tessa Thompson and Jon Bernthal, has viewers captivated with its dark twists and turns.
The show follows an estranged couple played by Thompson and Bernthal who set out to solve a murder mystery, with each believing the other is the prime suspect. But as the limited six-episode series plays out, we ultimately find Rebecca Rittenhouse’s character, newscaster Lexy Jones, guilty for the murder – only to later find out that she was framed. Unfortunately, despite her innocence, Lexy does not get a very happy ending.

Here’s everything to know about Rebecca Rittenhouse, who plays Lexy on His & Hers.
She wanted to become an actor when she was five

Rittenhouse was born in November 1988 in Los Angeles and grew up in Pasadena. In an interview with beauty website Into the Gloss, she revealed that her father’s love for classic films like Roman Holiday, High Society and To Catch a Thief inspired her to become an actress at the early age of five. Unfortunately, when she was approached while in high school by a manager seeking to represent her, she was asked to lose weight. “I enjoyed cheeseburgers too much to do that, and besides, education was also really important in my family, and by that point I thought I had to go down a more normal route in life,” she recalled.
Rittenhouse went on to pursue a degree in romance languages at the University of Pennsylvania, including French, Italian, Portuguese and Spanish.
She returned to acting after failed job interviews

During her senior year at university, Rittenhouse revealed in her Into the Gloss interview, she did several rounds of interviews for various jobs but told herself that if they failed she’d apply for acting school. Sure enough, Rittenhouse ended up attending the Atlantic Theater Company in New York City after graduating from UPenn in 2011.
She made her on-screen debut with a minor role in the 2014 TV series The Affair and went on to expand her portfolio with roles in various TV shows and films such as The Mindy Project, The Handmaid’s Tale, Once Upon a Time … in Hollywood, and Four Weddings and a Funeral.
Who is she dating?

Rittenhouse is engaged to Kyle Robiskie. The couple have reportedly been dating since late 2022 and announced their engagement via social media in 2023, with Rittenhouse sharing a photo of herself showing off her engagement ring with the caption, “Some questions aren’t hard to answer.”

Before dating Robiskie, Rittenhouse is known to have dated Gossip Girl alum, Chace Crawford.

data.title: ‘One of a kind’: tributes pour in for David Webb, Hong Kong’s voice of integrity
data.description:
Hong Kong activist investor David Webb, who has died aged 60, has been remembered for his integrity, courage and rigour as a “true pioneer” of the city’s corporate governance.
Tributes have poured in online for Webb, who died in Hong Kong on Tuesday of metastatic prostate cancer.
He revealed his diagnosis in 2020, saying at the time that one of his final wishes was to live beyond 60.
The Hong Kong Securities and Futures Professionals Association said on Wednesday that Webb was deeply respected in the city’s financial community for his fearless independence, unwavering integrity and relentless pursuit of transparency and justice.
Chairman Mofiz Chan described Webb’s death as a great loss to the industry, saying the association would honour his spirit by continuing to promote professionalism and integrity in the market.
“He adhered to his principles, spoke out courageously, and faced challenges head-on, consistently demonstrating a strong commitment to investor rights and market integrity, setting a professional standard,” Chan said in a statement.
He added that Webb’s platform, webb-site.com, which focused on corporate and economic governance, as well as business, finance, legal and regulatory affairs, had long provided valuable information to investors, professionals and the public.
“Its content was objective and insightful, offering significant reference value to industry peers and becoming an indispensable resource for daily work and decision-making,” he said.
“His contributions not only facilitated the exchange of industry knowledge but also promoted transparency and rational discussion of market information, leaving a profound impact on the healthy development of Hong Kong’s financial market.”
In 2017, Webb identified a group of interconnected Hong Kong-listed companies with suspicious cross-holdings and circular transactions, which he dubbed the “Enigma Network”.
That December, police raided the offices of Convoy Global Holdings in what became the city’s largest financial investigation in decades. Former executives and directors were arrested and many related stocks plummeted.
Tributes to Webb flooded social media, with many thanking him for making market information more transparent and for his contributions to Hong Kong’s financial sector.
On one platform, posts mourning his passing drew more than 300 comments and reposts, while on another users remembered him as an influential role model and tenacious watchdog.
The Accounting and Financial Reporting Council also paid tribute, describing Webb as a staunch advocate for transparency, accountability and good governance who fearlessly championed investor rights and exposed corporate malpractice.

Council chairman David Sun Tak-kei called him “one of a kind” – principled, fearless and deeply committed to doing what he believed was right.
“Having worked with him on the Listing Committee, I saw first-hand his passion for governance, transparency and accountability,” he said.
“He was truly one of a kind. His unwavering principles will continue to inspire and guide us in our shared mission.”
Finance sector lawmaker Ronick Chan Chun-ying said Webb protected small shareholders by exposing weaknesses in the management and finances of listed firms.
“Through his personal expertise and meticulous research, Webb highlighted the deficiencies in the business operations, financials and corporate governance of various listed companies for the benefit of retail investors,” he said.
“In doing so, he raised public awareness regarding corporate disclosure and contributed significantly to enhancing the reputation of Hong Kong’s capital market.”
A graduate of Exeter College, University of Oxford, Webb made significant philanthropic contributions to the institution. He established the Exeter College Incentive Scheme, endowing a fellowship, academic prizes and contributing hundreds of thousands of pounds.
David Ching, vice-president of Hong Kong’s CFA Society, said he was a fellow alumnus and a beneficiary of Webb’s philanthropy.
“His scholarships and prizes at Exeter have benefited many students like me, rewarding academic excellence and fuelling future leaders,” Ching wrote in a post on social media.
“Over the years, David generously shared his insights and wisdom that profoundly shaped my early career,” he added, saying Webb’s spirit inspired him to always question the status quo in corporate finance, mergers and acquisitions and capital markets.
Ching described Webb as a “true pioneer” in Hong Kong corporate governance, whose fearless advocacy for transparency inspired him to step into the finance world with a sharp analytical mind.
Through his database and exposés like the “Enigma Network”, he said Webb championed investor rights and unsettled complacency among the financial elite, reminding the industry that rigorous scrutiny is the bedrock of ethical markets.
He said he would remember Webb by his enduring words: “The stock exchange is not a casino; it’s a marketplace for information.”

data.title: Your Hong Kong weekend drinks guide for January 16-18
data.description:
We’re fresh into 2026, but the city’s nightlife scene is already building momentum as Peridot and Mius both host their first guest shifts of the year – the former featuring two Italian mixologists-slash-brand owners, and the latter featuring growing Singaporean collective Nutmeg & Clove.
Meanwhile, Hong Kong-inspired Kinsman – also an advocate for Chinatowns across the globe – celebrates two years in the city with a slew of collaborations from cha chaan teng favourites to Chinese dominoes.
Thursday, January 15
Peridot x Giancarlo Mancino x Simone Rossi

Peridot kicks off 2026 with a guest shift featuring two of the world’s foremost mixologists-turned-brand masterminds. Simone Rossi is the founder of Ratafia Rossi, a brand of cherry wine whose name refers to any liqueur infused with fruits. Before founding the brand, Rossi was part of DarkSide’s opening team, then director of bars at Rosewood Hong Kong until 2024.
Giancarlo Mancino is the developer of Mancino Vermouth as well as the founder of his own beverage consultancy firm and of Bocktailed canned cocktails. He also helped to develop Sei Bellissimi, which produces sparkling bottled cocktails.
Where: Summit 38, 38/F, The Henderson, 2 Murray Road, Central
When: 9pm-11pm
Friday, 16 January
Two Years of Kinsman

Kinsman celebrates two years of representing the flavours of Hong Kong and of Chinatowns across the world in cocktail form with a full weekend of collaborations. During the day and until January 18, the bar turns into Café Kinsman in a collaboration with Tai On Coffee and Tea Shop, serving cha chaan teng favourites such as a yuen yeung latte and egg tarts. In the evening, however, DJ Fabsabs and Johnnie Darka take over the decks to play Cantopop hits from 9pm onwards, before Tai Hang Tin9 Club and Clan & Company community bar take over Kinsman on January 18 for a day of Chinese dominoes and drinks inspired by the game.
Where: 65 Peel Street, Central
When: Saturday: 10.30am-4.30pm (Cafe Kinsman), 9pm-1am (A Good Ol’ Cantopop Night); Sunday: noon till late (Drop-in Dominoes), 6pm-9pm (Domino Drink Takeover)
Sunday, 18 January
Mius x Nutmeg & Clove

It’s a Singaporean Sunday at Mius as the bar hosts its first-ever guest shift to start the year. And who better to take over than founder Shelley Tai’s former group and concept, Singapore’s Nutmeg & Clove? Representing the Lion City are group founder Colin Chia and mixologist Chai Yu Heng of the flagship bar. Both look to bring Singapore-inspired flavours in both food and cocktail form.
Where: 29 Gough Street, Central
When: 7pm till late

data.title: Worker killed, 2 others injured by falling gas cylinders on Hong Kong building site
data.description:
A worker was killed and two others injured after three gas cylinders fell from height and struck them at a construction site in Tsuen Wan on Wednesday afternoon.
Police received a report at about 1.34pm that the cylinders fell as they were being hoisted by a crane and hit the three workers at the site on Po Fung Road.
Two of the men, aged 29 and 40, were struck by the cylinders and rushed to Princess Margaret Hospital in Kwai Chung for emergency treatment. The younger man was knocked unconscious and was certified dead at 2.40pm.
A third worker, 50, sustained minor injuries and was sent to Yan Chai Hospital in Tsuen Wan for treatment.
Police said two of the cylinders that fell measured about 153cm (five feet) in length and 21cm (8.2 inches) in width, while the third was 100cm (3.2 feet) long and 25cm (9.8 inches) wide.

Authorities have cordoned off the site and launched an investigation into the incident.
Separately, a male worker fell unconscious after being trapped by a board at a construction site on Shing Fung Lane in Kai Tak at about 4.20pm. He was taken to hospital for treatment.

data.title: Who are Iran’s Revolutionary Guards and what is their role in the crackdown on protesters?
data.description:
Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, or IRGC, is the ideological arm of Tehran’s military, answering to supreme leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, and stands accused by Western countries of orchestrating and perpetrating the crackdown on protesters.
Who are they?
The Guards, known as “Pasdaran” in Persian, were founded in 1979 by then supreme leader Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini “to propagate the ideas of the Islamic revolution”, said Clement Therme, a researcher at the International Institute of Iranian Studies.
“It’s an army of 150,000 to 180,000 people in service of an ideology.”
A Western diplomat who requested anonymity to speak said its membership was thought to be around 200,000.
“It’s an armed force that functions like an elite military with terrestrial, maritime and aerospace capabilities, while it is better trained, better equipped and better paid than the regular military,” the diplomat said.
The IRGC also serves as Tehran’s link to its regional allies, such as Hezbollah in Lebanon and pro-Iran militias in Iraq.
Its leadership is entirely appointed by the supreme leader.
Last June, Khamenei named Mohammad Pakpour as its head to replace Hossein Salami, killed on the first day of the recent war with Israel.
Pakpour is a veteran of the 1980-88 Iran-Iraq war.

What resources do they have?
“It’s an empire within an empire,” said David Khalfa, a researcher at the France-based Jean-Jaures Foundation.
The Guards control or own companies across the Iranian economy, including major strategic sectors.
They exercise near-monopolistic control of infrastructure, energy, technology, telecoms and finance, Khalfa said.
Their annual military budget is estimated at around US$6 billion to US$9 billion, or 40 per cent of Iran’s official military budget, according to data collated by Khalfa.
“They effectively control the Iranian economy,” he added.

How do they operate?
The Guards have put in place a vast intelligence network that is “the most extensive, the most effective in the Iranian regime,” Khalfa said.
In the past, they have proved capable of dismantling protest networks rapidly, identifying leaders within minutes.
To enforce their will, the Guards rely on the Basij paramilitary, which is recruited mainly from young Iranians and acts as an ideological organisation embedded in all institutions and levels of society.
There are thought to be around 600,000 to 900,000 Basij members, Khalfa said, pointing to cross-referenced data from several US think tanks.

What role do they have in repressing protests?
“They play a central role in the repression because today, more than ever, they are a pillar of the regime, a pillar of its continuity and its survival,” Khalfa said.
“They have a set political and operational line regarding the protests which is zero tolerance,” he added.
At least 2,571 have been killed during the protests, the US-based Human Rights Activists News Agency reported.
Experts believe that at the start of the demonstrations last month, the Guards were likely staying in the background and relying on local security forces and the Basij to handle the demonstrators.
However, “since the beginning, they have been steering the repressive apparatus,” Khalfa said.
With the protests defying that repression, the Guards sent their ground forces and their special units in, he added.
Therme, the researcher, said the Guards were operating in civilian clothing as part of efforts to deny their involvement in human rights violations.

Who considers them a terrorist organisation?
In 2019, the United States designated the Guards a terrorist organisation.
Australia designated it a state sponsor of terrorism in November, having blamed it for two 2024 arson attacks on its Jewish community.
In Europe there have been calls from officials and members of the European Parliament for the IRGC to be designated a terrorist group to increase pressure on Tehran.
A diplomatic source said Germany, in particular, would back listing the guards’ Quds Force, its elite overseas unit, which it has linked to a 2021 attack against a German synagogue.
Additional reporting by Associated Press

data.title: Philippines ‘comfort women’ demand apology during Japan foreign minister visit
data.description:
An advocacy group representing Filipino victims of wartime sexual slavery by Japanese soldiers will renew its decades-old demand for a formal state apology and reparations as Japan’s foreign minister visits the Philippines this week.
The group, Lila Pilipina (League of Filipino Grandmothers), said it plans to press its appeal during the visit of Foreign Minister Toshimitsu Motegi, who is set to arrive on Thursday.
“We demand a formal public apology and reparation based on the apology,” said Sharon Cabusao-Silva, a spokeswoman for the group.
However, analysts say Japan’s long-held position makes new reparations unlikely.

Tokyo maintains that all wartime claims were settled through post-war treaties, including the Philippines-Japan Reparations Agreement of 1956, and that Manila has waived all further claims on behalf of its citizens.
In the 1990s, Japan established the Asian Women’s Fund to provide “atonement payments” funded by private donations to “comfort women”, as the sex slaves were known, rather than from state coffers.
Many Filipino survivors rejected the payments, with advocates arguing the initiative lacked official acknowledgement of Japan’s legal responsibility.
“Even by the Japanese government’s own admission, it was not the official compensation,” Silva said. “The funds came from Japanese citizens and private corporations.”
However, she acknowledged that some members of her group had accepted payments from the fund out of economic necessity.
Of the more than 170 “comfort women” that Lila Pilipina has documented, only six are still alive.

A closed case?
The Philippine Department of Foreign Affairs has not confirmed whether the issue will be raised in Motegi’s talks with Foreign Secretary Theresa Lazaro.
The officials are expected to focus on regional security and Japan’s expanding role as a development and defence partner during their talks.
Edmund Tayao, a law professor at the San Beda Graduate School of Law in Manila, said the Philippine government would not prevent “any group” from raising the issue of reparations. “But they will also not support it,” he said.
Japan is the Philippines’ largest source of official development help, providing about US$13.23 billion in 2024 – roughly one-third of the total.
These funds have been used to finance flagship infrastructure projects, including the Metro Manila Subway, North-South Commuter Railway and Davao City Bypass.
Their policy is simply to ignore it
Sharon Cabusao-Silva, Lila Pilipina spokeswoman
Silva said this financial support had dissuaded Philippine governments “past and present” from raising the reparations issue with Japan.
“They cannot be relied upon to take up the case,” she said. “their policy is simply to ignore it.”
Legal experts note that Tokyo considers the matter closed and is unlikely to want to revisit it. “For the Japanese, that has always been a sensitive subject,” Tayao said.
The Philippine government had also waived its right to raise the issue, said Aldrin Alba of the Political Economic Elemental Researchers and Strategists think tank.
However, Alba noted that a United Nations women’s rights committee had previously ruled the Philippines violated victims’ rights by failing to provide reparations or recognition. Survivors of wartime sexual slavery could seek to enforce that decision in Philippine courts, he said.

Diane Desierto, a Filipino legal professor who served as co-counsel in that ruling, said there was “hesitation” in applying the ruling as it could open the “floodgates of litigation” for other wartime-era claims “long deemed settled”.
Japan’s stance on the issue has wavered over the decades. Initial apologies in the 1990s gave way to denials under former prime minister Shinzo Abe, who maintained that there was no proof of sexual coercion.
More recently, a Japanese diplomat visiting Manila in 2024 expressed “deep remorse” for the suffering inflicted on Filipino women during the war.
“The dignity and honour of those former comfort women was injured,” Japanese foreign ministry official Kaneko Mariko reportedly said in an interview.
“The government of Japan has been extending, expressing apologies and deep remorse for those actions during the war.”

data.title: LIV Golf’s Majestiks optimistic new look all-British line-up can benefit from rule changes
data.description:
With a new look for a new season and a LIV Golf with new rules, Majesticks GC co-captains Lee Westwood and Ian Poulter see a year of potential after the struggles of past campaigns.
An off-season when Laurie Canter opted to join as a replacement for Henrik Stenson rather than head to the PGA Tour, and saw Sam Horsfield return for another year, has given Majesticks an all-British line-up for the first time.
Coupled with the move to four round tournaments rather than the three that was one of LIV’s unique selling points, it is a quartet Poulter believes should have the group moving “up the team order like I feel we probably should”.
In truth, they could not do much worse. Majesticks finished 11th out of 13 teams last year and ninth the year before that, when they finished fourth in the stops in Hong Kong, Nashville and Chicago.
They ended the 2023 season, LIV’s first full year, 11th out of 12 teams and the second place in Bedminster in 2022 is still the only time they have made the podium.
Four rounds are hardly a guarantee things will improve, but Poulter points to performances on the final Sundays across 2025 as evidence that in several cases Majesticks were not as far behind as results might suggest.

“You know, last year and even the year before, we played well on Sunday, which means we were trending in the right direction and [then] the tournament finishes,” Poulter told the Post during LIV media week in Florida.
“You want to get off to that fast start in every single tournament you play, but three rounds seems like the tournament’s over when you just got going. So I’m hopeful that it’s good for Majesticks.”
They will all need to be on their games as individuals too, with relegation expanded to 20 per cent of the 57-man field, up from the six players, including Stenson, who lost their spots last year.
The Swede fell victim to a Poulter comeback for the ages at the event in Indianapolis, with the Englishman rattling off four birdies in his closing four holes to survive for another year.
Poulter welcomed the relegation change, believing it would “inevitably” lead to the awarding of world ranking points for LIV tournaments.
“It’s a shame we haven’t had them already or the news that we’re going to get them,” he said. “But look, it’s inevitable at some stage that they’re going to come.
“The fact 20 per cent of the guys will change at the end of the year and fall into the relegation zone means there is a pathway to bringing people back into the league.
“It’s hard for those people, and I’ve been close to that last year and managed to scrape out. So, there’ll be a few in that position and hopefully I’m not one of those.”
Despite the optimism, Westwood said they had yet to set any goals for the season, which begins next month in Riyadh.
The tour then heads to Adelaide and arrives in Hong Kong for the third time in the first week of March.
“We haven’t been particularly successful over the last couple of years, so we’re obviously looking to improve on those results and get podiums,” Westwood said. “But you’ve just got to start at the grass roots of it all. Start playing some good golf as a team, and shooting some low scores. And that [hopefully] snowballs into good results.”

Hong Kong is almost home away from home for Majesticks, who, among others, are sponsored by HSBC and sportswear brand Castore, which also provides the city’s rugby team with their kit.
The team’s rebrand, complete with British bulldog logo and a red, white and blue colour scheme, follows a theme running through the league over the past few weeks.
Stinger, a South African team, has evolved into Southern Guards – Rhino included – Iron Heads, minus the departed Kevin Na, have become Korean Golf Club, while Sergio Garcia has an entirely Spanish group of Fireballs for the first time.
When the league launched in 2022, Ollie Banks, one of the Majesticks’ co-team principals, said LIV “didn’t really want to geopin a lot of these teams”.
But it’s been one of CEO Scott O’Neil’s priorities since taking over last year, and “what’s happened is organically it’s kind of taken shape”.
